path: 99 Stuff/ETFA25 AAS Paper/ETFA25-000284 Toward Low-Code Industrial Data Integration through Asset Administration Shell-Based Flow Generation in Node-RED.pdf
text: Toward Low-Code Industrial Data Integration through Asset Administration Shell-Based Flow Generation in Node-RED Tam´as Farkas, Wei Guo, Ornella Mboudia, Igor Garmaev, Torben Miny, Tobias Kleinert Chair of Information and Automation Systems, RWTH Aachen University, Aachen, Germany {t.farkas, w.guo, o.mboudia, i.garmaev, t.miny, kleinert}@iat.rwth-aachen.de Abstract—Data integration remains a major challenge in the industry due to the heterogeneity of systems, data sources, and communication protocols. As the Asset Administration Shell becomes a widely adopted standard for digital representations, there is an increasing need for dynamic and user-friendly data integration solutions tailored to it. Existing methods often require extensive programming knowledge, which limits accessibility and slows down deployment. This work presents a low-code approach to industrial data integration based on the Asset Administration Shell, leveraging the standardized Asset Interfaces Description and Asset Interfaces Mapping Configuration submodels, and following an Extract, Transform, Load process. We introduce a Python-based extractor that reads metadata from Asset Inter- faces Description and Asset Interfaces Mapping Configuration submodels to automatically generate Node-RED flows with min- imal user input. This approach enables real-time data exchange by configuring communication nodes and mappings without manual programming. Overall, our work lowers the barriers to industrial data integration, empowers non-technical users to configure and deploy integration workflows more efficiently, and fosters interoperability through standardized data models. Index Terms—Asset Administration Shell, Asset Interfaces Description, Asset Interfaces Mapping Configuration, Node-RED, Flow Generation. I. INTRODUCTION In a world where companies compete fiercely and face increasing environmental pressures that demand more sus- tainable production, industries are being pushed to rethink traditional practices. A key response to these challenges is the advanced digitalization of industrial processes, which offers a significant competitive advantage. At the core of this digital transformation is the Asset Administration Shell (AAS), a standardized digital representation of industrial assets, initiated by the Plattform Industrie 4.0 initiative in Germany [1]. A persistent challenge in modern industrial systems is integrating heterogeneous data into a coherent structure [2]. The AAS addresses this by enforcing a consistent metamodel that structures asset data through standardized and proprietary submodels. Each submodel is created to cover a specific aspect of the asset. A well-constructed AAS is capable to represent the entire lifecycle of an asset serving as its digital twin. The standardization increases the interoperability between disparate systems, hence enabling development of abstraction layers that hide technical details, e.g. JavaScript Object Nota- tion (JSON) or Extensible Markup Language (XML) syntax, Application Programming Interface (API) documentation or interface configurations. As a result, even users without deep technical knowledge can seamlessly navigate, configure, and deploy AAS-based integrations, ensuring that asset data is correctly interpreted and connected with the attributes of the physical asset. While the AAS provides a structured way to represent asset- related information, extracting specific metadata, such as com- munication protocols, their configurations and connections, or updating AAS properties with real-time measurements can still require a certain level of expertise. To simplify this, we developed a Python-based tool that uses the AAS to retrieve the relevant data and automatically generate a Node-RED flow with already configured communication nodes based on standardized submodels. This reduces the required skill level for accessing real-time asset information and presents the flows of data in an easy-to-understand way. The remainder of this paper is structured as follows. Sec- tion II provides background on relevant technologies and standards, including Extract, Transform, Load (ETL) prin- ciples, AAS submodels (Asset Interfaces Description (AID) and Asset Interfaces Mapping Configuration (AIMC)), and current limitations in automation and flexibility. Section III reviews related works that combine AAS concepts with flow- based programming and automated flow generation, highlight- ing existing tools and platforms. Section IV introduces the conceptual foundation of our approach, outlining the envi- sioned workflow and automation strategy. Section V describes the implementation of the flow generation system and its validation, where the flow is generated from a sample AAS and tested with a simulated sensor. In Section VI, we critically discuss the strengths and limitations of the approach and outline potential enhancements, including future support for transformation standards, bidirectional synchronization, and usability improvement measures. Finally, Section VII summa- rizes the key contributions of this work. II. BACKGROUND Industrial data integration increasingly relies on standard- ized interfaces and low-code platforms to manage complexity. The AAS provides a semantic framework for describing assets, while tools like Node-RED offer a visual environment for designing data flows. This chapter outlines the key technolo- gies used in our approach, including Node-RED, relevant AAS submodels, and the ETL pattern, and discusses current limitations in automation and flexibility. A. Node-RED Low-code development platforms (LCDPs) aim to reduce the need for traditional hand-coded programming by provid- ing a component-centric and graphical development environ- ment [3]. These platforms enable users to create applica- tions through visual interfaces, making software development more accessible and efficient. This is particularly valuable in domains that require rapid prototyping and the integration of heterogeneous systems such as devices, communication protocols, and external services. Node-RED is one example of a flow-based low-code development tool that is widely used in the Internet of Things (IoT) and home automation domains [4]. It is built on the Node.js JavaScript runtime environment. A browser-based editor allows users to create applications by visually arranging and connecting nodes, forming executable programs known as flows. This approach resembles the idea of Flow-based programming (FBP), which models applications as networks of independent components that communicate by passing messages through predefined connections [5]. A core feature of FBP is its asynchronous nature. Node-RED supports this through its event-driven, non-blocking Node.js runtime, which enables nodes to process messages and forward results without blocking other tasks [6]. B. Submodels AID & AIMC The AID Submodel [7] standardizes how interfaces of asset- related services are described within the AAS. It includes essential details such as endpoint addresses, communication protocols (currently Modbus, Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP), Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (MQTT)), data formats, and security settings, facilitating straightforward access to data source. The AIMC Submodel [8] complements AID by defining how data from assets is mapped to specific elements within any target AAS submodel. It supports structured data integration, ensuring clear and accurate data handling for various applica- tions. However, the current AIMC version focuses solely on mapping and does not directly support data transformations. Thus, additional middleware or transformation tools are re- quired when complex data processing or transformations are necessary. C. Data integration pattern: ETL ETL is a fundamental data integration process that involves extracting data from heterogeneous sources, transforming it, and loading it into a target system [9]–[12]. The transformation step applies a sequence of functions to the extracted data, including cleaning, mapping, or modifying it (for example, by changing the Format) [9], [11]. During this step, one may also perform specific operations on the data [11], such as adding two properties extracted from the same or different AAS submodels. The transformation step is non mandatory, as some data may not require any transformation and can be directly moved or passed through [11], [12]. The value of the ETL concept for data integration in the AAS is demonstrated in [13], where an ETL framework is used to extract data from systems of record, transform the extracted data, and load it into an AAS or AAS descriptor. Earlier, [14] shows how data from an SAP database can be populated into the AAS repository via ETL and [15] proposed a technology-independent concept for integrating data into the AAS using an ETL algorithm. D. Limitations of Current Approaches Current AAS-based integration approaches, while providing a standardized semantic description, often result in rigid, point- to-point (1-to-1) data flows when directly mapped. These direct mappings, though useful for simple scenarios, inherently lack the flexibility and capability required for more complex many-to-many (m-to-n) source-sink connections, sophisticated data aggregation, or advanced transformation logic. The struc- ture of basic AAS mapping configurations, such as those described by the AIMC submodel in its current form, does not support these advanced integration patterns [8]. This creates a significant need for complementary tools and methodologies that can extend the capabilities of AAS-based systems. The limitations in handling complex data processing and fu- sion tasks motivate the exploration and integration of platforms like Node-RED, which offer a visual, flow-based approach to data manipulation and can effectively bridge these gaps, thereby enabling more versatile and powerful industrial data integration solutions. III. RELATED WORKS Several research efforts have explored the integration of flow-based programming tools with semantic technologies and model-driven approaches to facilitate industrial data in- tegration. In particular, the AAS has gained attention as a standardized interface for managing and exchanging asset- related information. This section reviews related works that demonstrate the use of AAS in flow-based environments such as Node-RED, and highlights approaches for the automated generation of Node-RED flows. An example for an AAS-based integration is described by Thuluva et al., who demonstrate how Node-RED flows can be automatically constructed by instantiating applica- tion templates with different device configurations [16]. To achieve this, both the device and application are described using semantic definitions based on iot.schema.org, providing the necessary information for a semantic matching tool that searches for specific devices to be inserted into the templates. Tajalli et al. built a system for detection of leaks in oil and gas pipelines [17]. They used the AAS as a central information source for collecting and storing the relevant properties of the pipe segments, and historical data based on measurements of IoT sensors. This data will be accessed by a real-time monitoring and notification system, which was implemented in Node-RED. Dhouib et al. presented a Model-Based Systems Engi- neering (MBSE) approach for implementing AAS in Pa- pyrus4Manufacturing (P4M) [18]. In P4M the structural ele- ments of the AAS can be defined using Unified Modeling Lan- guage (UML) class diagrams, and for each specific asset, the AAS is created using instance diagrams. Once the diagrams are complete, Java code can be generated, which runs an HTTP server where the AAS instances can be accessed with Create, Read, Update and Delete (CRUD) operations. Workflows are defined using Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN) diagrams, which can then be converted into Node-RED format. Several existing projects leverage traditional programming languages such as Python to generate code or domain-specific languages for visual and flow-based tools instead of manually constructing diagrams or flows. For example, PyFlowchart is a Python package which can produce flowchart.js 1 Domain- Specific Language (DSL), which is then transformed into static or interactive flowcharts by visual renderers [19]. Similarly, the Diagrams library lets architects describe cloud topologies in Python using simple operators to define connections be- tween components [20]. The resulting Python code generates Graphviz DOT 2 code that can be rendered as PNG or SVG images. In these cases, developers work entirely in Python and rely on libraries to programmatically produce the diagramming language, without writing the DSL by hand or using a graph- ical editor. The previously mentioned libraries generate visual repre- sentations, whereas other projects are designed to produce ex- ecutable programs for flow-based tools. Two notable examples are the Three-Way Flow Generator and FloWare, both generate Node-RED flows based on predefined flow definitions. The Three-Way Flow Generator3 is a Python/Flask application that automates the creation of three-way and parallel-switch logic for Home Assistant entities and emits Node-RED JSON flows that can be imported directly into the Node-RED editor [21]. FloWare-Core4 is an Electron-based platform whose ADOxx- powered graphical editor enables domain experts to configure sensors and actuators, select communication protocols and specify endpoint addresses, producing a structured XML fea- ture model that FloWare-Core then converts into a Node-RED- compatible JSON file [22]. Although these tools eliminate manual diagramming or GUI-based wiring, they still depend on explicit, predefined mappings. This means nodes can only be generated based on fixed structures, limiting flexibility. In more complex scenarios such rigid mappings would constrain extensibility. The Eclipse BaSyx open-source initiative provides a scal- able microservice, called DataBridge, designed for the versa- tile integration of various data sources with the AAS submod- els [23]. Its underlying architecture leverages Apache Camel, a message-oriented middleware, which provides a foundation 1 https://flowchart.js.org/ 2 https://graphviz.org/doc/info/lang.html 3 https://github.com/guimatheus92/node-red-homeassistant-three-way 4 https://github.com/PROSLab/FloWare-Core for connecting systems that produce or consume data and natively offers support for a wide array of data sources, such as HTTP and MQTT. So the DataBridge is also capable of sourcing data from these protocols. The DataBridge utilizes flexible integration patterns, including both time-triggered and event-based methods, to foster efficient and adaptable data exchange. Tools like JSONata can be employed to process and standardize incoming data streams before they are conveyed to the designated submodels through their HTTP/REST API. Additionally, the system features a delegation mechanism, configured by applying qualifiers to the relevant AAS model element, which enables the reading of property values and the invocation of operations over HTTP. This design ensures that the DataBridge can operate with any system compliant with the AAS specification, avoiding dependency on specific Software Development Kits (SDKs) or implementations. For managing its setup, the DataBridge uses adaptable configura- tion files, and their generation can be streamlined through a Node-RED-based GUI [23]. One further tool for data integration is the Neoception Digi- tal Twin Infrastructure, which facilitates the dynamic, context- based, and automated generation of AAS, supporting both on- demand creation and repository-based provisioning [24]. Its four-layer architecture systematically handles data integration: the Connection Layer gathers and bundles data from diverse sources; the Standardization Layer transforms proprietary data for interoperability; the Modelling Layer enables rule-based creation and selection of AAS templates; and the Application Layer provides interfaces (e.g., AAS-Viewer, API) to access the digital twins. Key to this infrastructure is a Submodel Repository for managing and utilizing Submodel templates, including standardized ones. Template Rules then dictate the application of specific template groups to assets or asset categories based on defined contexts and conditions. Finally, Data Rules enable the seamless mapping of an organization’s proprietary data onto standardized AAS elements, a configu- ration typically set up once and then applied automatically. The AID and AIMC plugin [25], developed by the Industrial Digital Twin Association (IDTA) for the AASX Package Explorer, supports the integration of live data into AASs based on the AID and AIMC submodel templates. It enables connections to the protocols supported by the official AID specification [7]. When an AAS file containing an AID and AIMC submodel is opened using the AASX Package Explorer with the plugin active, the plugin interprets these submodels at runtime, initializes the corresponding data sources, and automatically writes incoming values into the defined AAS elements. The main advantage of the plugin is its ability to easily connect live data sources to an AAS using standard protocols and simple mappings, all directly inside the AASX Package Explorer. However, one limitation is that it handles only the extraction and loading of data, without offering a transformation step. The BaSyx DataIntegrator is an open-source and Docker- deployable AAS data integration tool designed to integrate data from legacy systems into an AAS or its descriptor using an ETL approach [26]. The implementation is based on open-source tools such as Eclipse BaSyx, Spring Batch, Spring Integration, and JSONata. The ETL steps are modeled using a submodel-based configuration similar to the AID submodel. In the Extract submodel, the configuration specifies the data source, the mapper type, and the mapping method. The mapper type specifies whether the data is stored as a simple key-value pair, a list of values under a single key, or as a key-value pair where both key and value come directly from data source attributes. In the first two cases, the key is arbitrarily defined by the user and mapped to one or more values from the data source. In the Transform submodel, a type processor is used to convert the data types of the extracted values into user-configurable types. The Load submodel defines the target structure and the way in which the extracted and transformed data is written into the AAS or its descriptor. Finally, there is an integrator pipeline, also defined as a submodel, which references the three ETL submodels. The structured and submodel-based approach implemented in the Basyx DataIntegrator enables the flexible definition and orchestration of ETL processes within AAS environments. However, the current implementation is limited to Structured Query Language (SQL) and HTTP data sources, which re- stricts integration scenarios involving other protocols or data formats. IV. CONCEPT This section introduces the central concept underlying our approach. By using AAS metadata as input, we automatically generate executable data integration flows in Node-RED. This bridges the gap between semantic asset descriptions and real- world data connectivity, making complex integration tasks accessible to users with minimal coding. A. Vision: Simplifying Industrial Data Integration To address the limitations in flexibility and complex data processing capabilities identified in current AAS based inte- gration approaches, particularly concerning m-to-n data fusion as discussed in Section II-D, this work aims to significantly reduce the effort and specialized expertise typically required for integrating diverse industrial data sources. The primary goal is to leverage versatile data processing and visualization platforms, with a specific focus on Node-RED, to complement AAS-based semantic descriptions. This research seeks to enable more sophisticated and adaptable integration scenarios by automating the generation of foundational data flows from standardized AAS metadata. A key aspect of this vision is the adoption of a low-code approach. By automating the creation of the fundamental flow structure directly from AAS metadata, specifically the AID and AIMC submodels, the system empowers users, even those with minimal programming experience, to generate functional and deployable data integration flows. This, in turn, lowers the barrier to entry for advanced industrial data integration tasks, facilitating a move beyond simple point-to-point connections towards more complex and value-added data utilization. B. Workflow for AAS-to-Node-RED Flow Generation The proposed automated workflow assumes that users have either already configured the necessary data points using the AIMC submodel or possess the knowledge to do so. The overview of the proposed system centers on an au- tomated workflow designed to transform standardized AAS metadata, specifically information contained within AID and AIMC submodels, into executable Node-RED flows. This process is initiated with minimal input from the user: the Internationalized Resource Identifier (IRI) of the target AIMC submodel and the Uniform Resource Locator (URL) of the AAS submodel server where the relevant AID and AIMC instances are hosted. The workflow can be conceptualized in the following stages: 1) Stage 1: Initiate configuration: The process commences with the user providing the necessary configuration data, which includes information pertaining to the AIMC and the AAS server. Subsequently, the system retrieves the specified AIMC data based on this input. Following this, the corresponding AID data is retrieved, typically derived from or linked through the AIMC. An initial validation is then performed to ensure that these submodels are accessible, and their integrity is satisfactory for subsequent processing. 2) Stage 2: Generate Source & Target Nodes: After suc- cessful metadata ingestion, the AIMC is processed to extract the defined mappings between data sources and sinks. Con- currently, the AID is processed to extract the interface details for the entities involved in these mappings, such as commu- nication protocols and endpoint information. An optional step may be performed to filter these potential connections based on predefined criteria, such as supported protocols or specific user requirements. This stage culminates in the definition of all necessary parameters for the subsequent generation of flows. 3) Stage 3: Configure Flow: With the node parameters defined, flow nodes corresponding to these parameters are generated within the target environment’s paradigm (in this paper, Node-RED nodes). Connections between these newly generated nodes are then programmatically established, reflect- ing the mappings extracted in the previous stage. To enhance the usability and clarity of the resulting flow, an automated layout algorithm is typically applied to arrange the nodes and connections in a visually organized manner, as shown in the right part of Fig. 1. The generated flow is structured into three main regions: the left section represents the source side, and the right section corresponds to the sink side. Both sections contain communication nodes, as well as additional functions for preprocessing data and configuring interfaces. These two regions are automatically generated and should not be modified by the user. In contrast, the central section is in- tended for user customization, allowing the addition of custom processing functions and the rearrangement of connections. This flexibility enables the realization of m-to-n mappings and the integration of additional communication protocols. 4) Stage 4: Generate & Deploy: Finally, a deployable rep- resentation of the configured flow is generated in standardized JSON format. This deployable artifact is then imported by the Fig. 1. Transformation from the AAS interface metadata to the ready Node-RED flow via the internal Python data model user into the target execution environment (in this paper, a Node-RED instance). Following the import, the user deploys the flow, enabling its execution, and can further customize or extend it as required to meet specific operational needs. It is important to note that while the AIMC submodel is central to the presented automated workflow for defining mappings, the AID submodel can also be utilized indepen- dently in other scenarios. For instance, the user could use an AID to generate source nodes and then manually create target nodes and configure the flow logic directly within Node-RED. However, such alternative approaches that involve significant manual flow configuration beyond the AAS-driven automation are considered outside the scope of this paper, which focuses on maximizing automation through the combined use of AID and AIMC. C. Node-RED-Library The library consists of two main components: nodes and flows. A flow can be either a regular flow represented by the Flow class or a subflow. A subflow is modeled in the Subflow class, which inherits from the Flow class. Thus, a subflow is a specialization of a flow. A subflow encapsulates parts of the Node-RED flow, enabling a modularized and reusable structure. After a subflow is created, it can be added to any flow or subflow as a regular node [27]. Throughout this section, the term flow is used generically to refer to both flows and subflows, unless a distinction is explicitly necessary. Fig. 2 provides an overview of the library’s class structure, highlighting the relationships between these components. Usually, flows are configured using the Node-RED web interface, where nodes are chosen from palettes and placed on the flow by drag and drop, with their position set where the mouse is released. In the generated flow, every node should have a position given by the flow generator, otherwise, the nodes will be stacked on each other at position (0, 0). To address this problem, a grid is defined in every flow, where the nodes will be automatically placed on the grid points. The flow is configured with a list of the columns’ horizontal positions along with the vertical spacing. To place a node, it must be associated with its column number, so each column is filled with equidistant vertical spacing from top to bottom. A ghost node, i.e., a placeholder, can be added to maintain the spacing for larger structures, in case an ETL process occupies multiple rows of the grid. All nodes inherit from the abstract NodeBase class. It defines the basic structure that every node must have and provides a method to convert a node object into the required JSON format. The classes Node and ConfigNode are inherited from NodeBase. The difference between these two classes is that Node has a visual representation on a flow, while a ConfigNode is used by instances of the Node class for their configuration, so they do not show up on any flow. These base classes can be further extended to define spe- cific node types that will be instantiated within the flows. For example, MQTTBroker(ConfigNode), MQTTIn(Node), and HTTPRequest(Node) are subclasses that correspond to actual node types available in Node-RED. After the nodes have been created and configured, they can be added to a Flow object and connected with each other. Furthermore, subflows can be inserted into flows. If the flow is ready, a JSON representation of it will be generated for importing into Node-RED. D. AID/AIMC-Extractor The AID/AIMC-Extractor is responsible for reading, inter- preting, and transforming metadata stored in AAS submodels into deployable Node-RED flows. It serves as a bridge between structured metadata in the form of AID and AIMC submodels and the executable logic used for data processing. Fig. 1 illustrates the conversion process: the red path represents the Fig. 2. Class hierarchy of the Node-RED-Library source, the blue path represents the sink, and the AASInterface subflow is inserted into each channel and highlighted in green. Its constituent nodes are enclosed within a green frame. On the left side of the figure, the MQTT submodel element collection of the AIMC submodel is displayed in the BaSyx AAS Web UI 5. The collection contains references to the source configuration defined in the AID submodel and to the sink, which corresponds to a property in the OperationalData sub- model. At the top of the figure, an intermediate representation of these references is shown as a Python dictionary. This structure serves as the basis for generating the Node-RED flow depicted below it. At the bottom of the figure, the uniform AASInterface subflow, also generated by the AID/AIMC- Extractor, is shown. The doubled HTTP request nodes are required due to the lack of native support for PATCH requests, necessitating a workaround via multiple sequential calls. During the metadata extraction process from the AAS, the script makes API requests to the AAS submodel server and retrieves the submodels’ JSON representation. To interpret this data structure, the script performs a sequential traversal of the JSON tree, navigating between submodel elements. During this traversal, it identifies key junctions by detecting recurring structural patterns and matching semantic identifiers. Once the relevant elements are located, the system proceeds to collect all source-sink relations. Subsequently, the interface descriptions are extracted from the AID submodel. Based on this information, the system identifies the communication protocols involved and applies relevant filters. For instance, only connections using the MQTT protocol may be selected for inclusion in the Node-RED flow. The script then iterates through the filtered connections, extracts the corresponding configuration metadata from the AID submodel, and generates the required node objects and their interconnections using the Node-RED-Library. The resulting flow can then be imported into Node-RED and executed. V. IMPLEMENTATION The implemented code is built in a modular fashion using class hierarchies for ease of use and further development. The two main parts of the system, the Node-RED-Library and the AID/AIMC-Extractor, are both implemented in Python. 5 https://github.com/eclipse-basyx/basyx-aas-web-ui The implementation was validated using a representative example AAS that includes an OperationalData submodel, an AID submodel supporting three distinct communication protocols, and an AIMC submodel, which establishes con- nections between properties in the OperationalData submodel and the interface configurations defined in the AID. This AAS was uploaded to a repository, making it accessible to the AID/AIMC-Extractor. Based on this setup, the system successfully generated Node-RED flows (Fig. 1), which were deployed without requiring any manual modifications. Fig. 3 illustrates an example data integration process using a simu- lated sensor that publishes values via MQTT. The generated Node-RED flow channels these values into the Operational- Data submodel of the AAS. As shown on the right side of the figure, the current state of the submodel reflects the most recently simulated values, demonstrating the correct end-to- end integration of the sensor data pipeline. VI. DISCUSSION The presented work demonstrates the feasibility of auto- mated flow generation for data integration based on standard- ized metadata in the context of the AAS. By combining a purpose-built Python library for flow creation with a dedicated extractor for AID and AIMC submodels, the system achieves a high degree of automation, modularity, and reusability. A significant emphasis on automation is placed on this core workflow. Once the initial inputs are provided, the system aims to automate the structural generation of the flow as much as possible, minimizing manual intervention. Furthermore, while automation is a key focus, the current scope and inherent limitations of this approach should be acknowledged. The primary focus remains on the automated generation of the Fig. 3. Integration of simulated sensor values into the AAS using an MQTT broker as a live data source basic flow structure and connectivity, as derived directly from the AID and AIMC submodels. More complex logic, such as intricate data transformation rules or many-to-many data fu- sion operations, are generally considered to be post-generation manual configurations performed by the user directly within the Node-RED environment. This is partly because the current AIMC specification does not offer extensive, standardized support for defining such complex transformations. A notable strength of the implementation lies in its usability: users only need to provide two parameters—the IRI of the AIMC submodel and the URL of the AAS server—to generate a complete flow. This makes the system suitable even for non- experts. Furthermore, the grid-based flow layout ensures that the generated flows are not only functional but also visually interpretable within the Node-RED interface. However, some limitations can be identified. The first is that any change to the AID or AIMC submodels requires a complete re-execution of the flow generation process. As a consequence, all manual modifications made in the Node-RED editor, such as adding transformation logic, renaming nodes, changing colors, or repositioning elements, are lost and must be redone after each regeneration. This behavior can signifi- cantly hinder usability and discourage users from customizing or refining generated flows. Second, the handling of invalid or unexpected submodel content is minimal. The system assumes that the AAS and its submodels are well-formed and conform to the respective metamodel and submodel specifications. It does not perform explicit checks for structural or semantic compliance, nor does it validate whether the submodels fol- low the required schemas. As a result, malformed or non- conformant inputs may lead to failures during flow generation without meaningful feedback or diagnostics, which could be critical in production environments. The implementation of the Node-RED-Library was designed to support fully automated flow generation. However, at this stage, there is no need to offer advanced functionalities. As a result, the NodeBase class does not account for advanced interface configurations, such as authentication mechanisms, access controls, or other custom settings defined at the node level. Although the classes are structured to allow instantiation using an arbitrary dictionary, this process requires familiarity with the internal workings of Node-RED. Specifically, the dictionary should contain the JSON representation of the corresponding Node-RED object. The AIMC submodel is crucial for the presented automated flow generation, as it defines the essential mappings between source and target data points. Currently, however, the AIMC specification primarily supports one-to-one (1-to-1) data ele- ment mappings. Future revisions of the AIMC specification should therefore consider incorporating explicit support for m-to-n mapping capabilities. Introducing semantic constructs to define aggre- gation functions (e.g., sum, average), data splitting rules, or conditional mapping logic directly within the AIMC would be highly beneficial. Enhancing the AIMC to natively support these complex relationships would significantly increase the automation potential for generating sophisticated data integra- tion flows. A crucial area for advancement, and a key area for ex- tending system functionality in response to future updates of the AAS specification, lies in the automated generation of data transformation logic. As the AAS specifications evolve, particularly if a dedicated submodel for data transformations is introduced, these could be leveraged to automatically insert and configure appropriate transformation nodes (e.g., Node- RED function nodes or specialized data mapping nodes) between sources and sinks. This would address a key cur- rent limitation and significantly reduce the need for manual post-generation customization, especially for common ETL tasks. Similarly, if the AID submodel is extended to support additional protocols such as Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture (OPC UA), these should be integrated into the tool to maintain compatibility and expand the range of supported communication technologies. A promising extension of the system would be to support the conversion in the opposite direction: from an existing Node-RED flow back into AAS submodels. This would enable non-technical users to configure communication logic directly within the visual Node-RED interface, without needing to understand the structure or syntax of the AAS. Instead of manually editing submodels using specialized AAS editors, users could define interfaces and connections through intuitive drag-and-drop workflows within the Node-RED environment. The generated flows would then be automatically transformed into valid AID and AIMC submodels. When combined with the current work, this bidirectional transformation could enable full synchronization between the AAS and the Node-RED flow. In this setup, the AAS sub- models would serve as the single source of truth, while the Node-RED flow would function as both a user interface and a live reflection of the system’s current integration state. This approach would support real-time customization, provide transparency for system updates triggered elsewhere in the AAS, and create a more intuitive environment for managing industrial data flows. Finally, investigating more intelligent flow generation, pos- sibly incorporating machine learning to suggest or optimize integration patterns based on historical data or common use cases, represents a long-term vision. Such advancements would further reduce configuration burden and enhance the adaptability of generated integration solutions. These future enhancements aim to progressively minimize manual interven- tion, moving toward a fully automated, low-code environment for industrial data integration based on AAS and Node-RED. To pursue these goals, we are currently planning an open- source initiative in collaboration with academic institutions and industrial partners in Germany. The aim of this initiative is to establish a shared development framework for a com- prehensive and extensible data integration system based on open standards. Participating members will be committed to advancing interoperability, transparency, and practical usabil- ity in industrial data-driven environments. This effort aligns with the overarching objectives of Industry 4.0 by promoting standardized, modular, and easily integrable solutions for the digitalization of industrial processes [28]. VII. CONCLUSION AND OUTLOOK This work presented an approach for automated Node- RED flow generation from standardized interface descriptions based on the AAS. The implementation comprises two main components: a Python library for flow creation and an ex- tractor for interpreting AID and AIMC submodels. Together, these components enable seamless transformation of metadata into deployable and adjustable integration logic, significantly lowering the barrier to configuring asset communication. Looking ahead, several directions for future development have been identified. A key area is the extension of the system’s functionality in response to future updates of the AAS specification. For instance, if a dedicated submodel for data transformations is introduced, it could be used to automate the currently manual configuration of the transformations between sources and sinks. Similarly, if the AID submodel is extended to support additional protocols such as OPC UA, these should be integrated into the tool to maintain compatibility and expand the range of supported communication technologies. Other potential enhancements include improved error handling and mitigation strategies to make the system more robust against malformed or incomplete submodels, as well as the introduction of bidirectional synchronization between Node- RED flows and AAS submodels to support a more interactive and user-friendly configuration process, where changes in either environment could be reflected in the other.
